sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 12, level 1.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at row 38-39, col 28 in lower blue area. RIGHT moves player ~6 cols right. Green blocks (N) are in black areas, not directly walkable. Need to understand how to interact with them. MOUSE and ACTION7 are untested.
- Goal model: Navigate the player through the blue maze to collect all green blocks.
- Action model: LEFT/RIGHT likely move the player horizontally. Need to test UP/DOWN or other navigation.
- Plan: First probe movement with LEFT and RIGHT to understand controls, then map out a path to collect green blocks.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 11 of about 21 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have a clearer picture:
- Initial player: col 22
- Current player: col 34 (after 2 RIGHTs, each moving ~6 cols)
- 7 green blocks initially at:
  - Row 1-5: cols 14-16, 20-22, 26-28 (3 blocks in top area)
  - Row 13-17: cols 32-34, 38-40, 44-46, 50-52 (4 blocks in middle area)
- White bar at row 63, cols 0-63 (64 pixels) initially - this is a full-width bar at the bottom
- Purple bar appeared later (cols 0-10, 11 pixels) - seems to track something
I notice the green blocks are at specific columns. The player starts at col 22, which aligns with the second green block in the top row (cols 20-22). Maybe the player needs to navigate to align with each green block, then use some action to collect it?
But I already clicked all green blocks with MOUSE and they turned blue. The level didn't complete though.
Wait - let me reconsider. Maybe the game requires:
1. Player navigates to the correct column (aligned with a green block)
2. Player uses ACTION7 or MOUSE to "collect" the green block
3. The green block disappears when collected properly
But when I clicked with MOUSE, the green blocks turned blue (not disappeared). And ACTION7 seemed to undo the last click.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe:
- MOUSE converts green blocks to blue (this is just painting/interacting)
- The player needs to navigate to specific positions
- ACTION7 is used to "submit" or "advance" when the player is at the right position
Or maybe the goal is for the player to visit all the green block locations. Since the player can only move LEFT/RIGHT, it can only visit positions in the lower blue area. The green blocks are in the upper areas, so the player can't reach them directly.
Hmm, let me look at this from a different angle. The white bar at the bottom (64 pixels) might be a "remaining steps" or "progress" bar. The purple bar might indicate completed actions.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole approach. Maybe I should:
1. Undo all my MOUSE clicks using ACTION7 (to restore the green blocks)
2. Then try a different approach where the player navigates to each green block's column and uses a different action
But that would waste many actions. Let me first try to understand what ACTION7 does more carefully.
Actually, let me try something: move the player to align with one of the green block columns, then use ACTION7 to see what happens.
The green blocks are at columns:
- Top row: 14-16, 20-22, 26-28
- Middle row: 32-34, 38-40, 44-46, 50-52
The player is currently at col 34, which aligns with the first middle-row green block (cols 32-34). Let me try ACTION7 from this position.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model updated: Player started at col 22, now at col 34. 7 green blocks were at specific columns. MOUSE converts green to blue. ACTION7 appears to undo last MOUSE click. White bar (64px) and purple bar (growing) at bottom are HUD elements. Level not complete after clicking all greens. Need to understand if player position matters.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is at col 34, aligned with green block at cols 32-34
# Let me try ACTION7 from this position to see if it does something different
# First, let me check if there are any green blocks remaining
seg = current_frame.segmentation
green_count = sum(1 for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N')
print(f"Green blocks remaining: {green_count}")

# Try ACTION7
action(['ACTION7'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
